title: The Best Movie of 2021 So Far, and a Chuck Klosterman Theory of Film
format: Chuck Klosterman stops by with two ideas: a defense of Adam Curtis's sprawling BBC documentary Can't Get You Out of My Head, and a long-standing theory about whether the movies a character has supposedly seen also exist in the reality of the movie they're in.

segments.heading: Adam Curtis and "Can't Get You Out of My Head"

films.title: HyperNormalisation
films.note: Curtis's shorter, less abstract predecessor to "Can't Get You Out of My Head"; Klosterman watched it after starting the new series.

segments.heading: The Chuck Klosterman Theory: Do Movies Exist Within Movies?
segments.note: if a character in a movie could watch other movies, which ones exist in their reality?

films.title: The Beach
films.note: Klosterman's test case: does Titanic exist in the world of this movie, and if so, who plays the lead?

films.title: Titanic
films.note: If it exists in the reality of The Beach, Klosterman argues Matthew McConaughey plays the lead instead of DiCaprio.

films.title: Contact
films.note: Part of Klosterman's chain reaction: if McConaughey is in Titanic instead, does Ethan Hawke get this role instead?

films.title: The Newton Boys
films.note: Another link in the McConaughey/Hawke recasting chain Klosterman walks through.

films.title: Dazed and Confused
films.note: Cited as the movie that would have sent people to see Contact instead, in Klosterman's alternate-casting chain.

films.title: Last Action Hero
films.note: Schwarzenegger plays a character who steps out of a movie into the "real world" of the film, then meets the real Arnold; Klosterman's favorite example of the concept.

films.title: Fight Club
films.note: Raised for a street scene with a poster for Seven Years in Tibet.

films.title: Seven Years in Tibet
films.note: The movie on the poster glimpsed in Fight Club.

films.title: Ocean's Twelve
films.note: Fennessey's pick for the movie that most aggressively confronts this concept: it posits a woman named Tess who looks exactly like Julia Roberts, so she can be used as a decoy for the real Julia Roberts.

films.title: Notting Hill
films.note: Fennessey's counter-example: Julia Roberts plays a movie star who is essentially Julia Roberts, the same question turned around.

films.title: Godzilla vs. Kong
films.note: Klosterman brings up the then-upcoming release (referred to on air as "King Kong versus Godzilla") to ask whether the old Godzilla movies exist as historical fact in its world.

films.title: Footloose
films.note: Klosterman's example of a closed-world movie with no other movies in it, though the hero's memories of dancing in Chicago complicate that.

films.title: I'm Thinking of Ending Things
films.note: Fennessey raises its parody of a fictional Robert Zemeckis movie as another version of the question.

films.title: Being John Malkovich
films.note: Cited as a film whose internal rules simply have to be accepted, the way Star Trek's speed rules are.

films.title: Scream
films.note: The literal version of the theory: characters survive because they know the rules of horror movies from having watched them.

films.title: Once Upon a Time in Hollywood
films.note: DiCaprio's character appears in fictional TV shows alongside real ones, and the film's ending diverges from real history entirely: what else is different in that world?

films.title: The Doors
films.note: Oliver Stone's Jim Morrison biopic; a recent Rewatchables episode on it is what launches Klosterman's biopics tangent.

films.title: I'm Not There
films.note: Klosterman's preferred model for biopics: an interpretive, non-chronological approach to a life rather than cradle-to-grave.

films.title: The Butterfly Effect
films.note: Cited by Fennessey at the close as "the movie that explains this concept" of small changes cascading into different realities.

TV: Can't Get You Out of My Head

Once Upon a Time in Hollywood.title: Once Upon a Time... in Hollywood
